what: what is this in arrow function
why: why arrow function doesnt have this
why is this undefined in arrow method
how: how to fix this in settimeout
compare: arrow function vs regular function this
output: arrow function in object returns window

1_beginner_explanation.summary: **Arrow functions do NOT have their own `this` keyword.** Period. If you use `this` inside an arrow function, it behaves exactly like a normal variable lookup: it looks up into the surrounding code (lexical scope) to find the nearest `this` from the parent function. Because of this, arrow functions are terrible for object methods, but absolutely perfect for callbacks like `setTimeout` or `.map()`.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: A regular function is like a self-centered actor: when it says 'this', it refers to whoever hired it. An arrow function is like a parrot: it doesn't have an ego, it just repeats whatever 'this' meant to the person who created the parrot.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When the engine creates an Execution Context for an Arrow Function, it does NOT create a `ThisBinding` record. When the code inside the arrow function evaluates the word `this`, it triggers standard Lexical Scope Resolution, traversing up the Scope Chain until it finds a parent environment that actually has a `ThisBinding`.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Because arrow functions don't create `this` bindings or `arguments` objects, they are slightly more memory-efficient than regular functions.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Fixing the classic 'this is lost in setTimeout' callback bug.

4_common_confusion.trap: Using an arrow function as an object method. If you do `speak: () => { console.log(this.name) }`, the arrow function looks UP to the global scope to find `this`. It will print undefined.
4_common_confusion.fix: Rule of thumb: Never use arrow functions as top-level object methods. Always use them as nested callbacks.

6_edge_case.quirk: Because arrow functions don't have a `this`, you cannot use `.bind()`, `.call()`, or `.apply()` to manually force a `this` context onto them. The engine will completely ignore your attempt.
